content:
6 min read
Just now

--

Here’s a situation. You’re blindfolded, standing somewhere in the middle of a hilly mountain range. Your job is to find the lowest valley. You can’t see anything. You can’t jump to the answer. The only thing you can do is feel the slope directly under your feet and take one step in whichever direction feels most downhill.
You repeat that. Feel the slope. Take a step. Feel the slope. Take a step. Eventually, after enough steps, you settle into a valley somewhere below. You might not know if it’s the deepest valley in the entire range, but you know you’ve gone downhill consistently, and you’ve stopped somewhere that feels like the bottom. That is gradient descent, and it is the engine underneath virtually every machine learning model that exists.
The Problem It’s Solving
When a neural network is trained, it starts with a completely random set of internal parameters — the numbers that control how it weighs different inputs and combines signals across its layers. At that starting point, the model makes terrible predictions. It gets almost everything wrong. The gap between what it predicted and what the correct answer actually is can be measured as a number called the loss. A high loss means the model is very wrong. A low loss means the model is performing well.
The goal of training is to identify the specific combination of parameter values that produces the lowest possible loss. Here’s the challenge: a typical neural network has millions of parameters. You cannot try every possible combination. Even if you could list every possible value for every parameter and test them all, the mathematics of that search space is so vast it’s practically incomprehensible. There’s no formula that tells you the right answer directly. You can only calculate, at your current position in that vast space, which direction is downhill.
That’s the gradient. It’s a vector, a direction that points toward the steepest uphill slope at the current position. And gradient descent moves in the opposite direction: downhill, one small step at a time.
The Learning Rate: The Most Important Dial on the Machine
Once you know which direction is downhill, you still have to decide how big a step to take. That decision is controlled by a number called the learning rate, and it’s the most consequential hyperparameter in the whole training process.
Set it too high, and each step overshoots the valley. The model jumps past the low point, up the other side, back down, and past it again, oscillating wildly without ever settling. The loss might explode rather than decrease, or it might bounce unpredictably forever. Set it too low and you’re taking such tiny steps that training takes an enormous amount of time. You’re moving in the right direction, but so slowly that reaching anything useful might take prohibitively long.
The practical intuition is as follows: if you’re descending a mountain in thick fog and can only see two meters in front of you, you take careful, small steps. Large steps carry you off the edge. If you don’t take any steps, you stay exactly where you started. The right step size depends on how steep and uneven the terrain is, and choosing it well is more art than science, which is why learning rate is one of the first things experienced practitioners adjust when training isn’t going as expected.
Mini-Batches: The Practical Compromise
Training on every single example in the dataset before taking one step (called batch gradient descent) produces a very accurate sense of direction but is impractically slow for large datasets. Training on a single random example and immediately taking a step (called stochastic gradient descent) is fast but so noisy that the path to the valley looks like a random walk rather than a controlled descent.
The standard approach in modern deep learning is mini-batch gradient descent: process a small group of examples, typically 32 to 256, at once. Compute the gradient across that batch, and then take one step. This gives you a reasonably accurate sense of direction, fast enough to be practical, noisy enough to sometimes help escape flat regions or shallow bad spots in the landscape. Nearly every model you’ll encounter in production was trained this way.
Adam: Why Nobody Uses Vanilla Gradient Descent Anymore
Plain gradient descent, while conceptually clean, has been largely superseded in practice by a family of more sophisticated optimizers. The most widely used of these is Adam, which stands for Adaptive Moment Estimation, though the name matters less than understanding what it does.
Adam keeps track of two things simultaneously: the average direction the gradients have been pointing recently (useful for building up momentum in consistent directions, similar to how a rolling ball speeds up) and the average size of gradients for each individual parameter (useful for adjusting step sizes). parameters that have been updated a lot get smaller steps, while parameters that barely changed get larger steps. The combination makes Adam remarkably robust across a wide range of problems. The default settings work well without careful tuning, which is why “start with Adam and a learning rate of 0.001” is genuinely good practical advice for almost any new deep learning project.
Reading What’s Actually Happening During Training
One of the most practically valuable skills in machine learning is learning to read training curve graphs that plot training loss and validation loss over time. The patterns tell you almost everything about what’s going wrong and how to fix it.
Training loss is decreasing alongside validation loss, both tracking close to each other: healthy training. Training loss is decreasing while validation loss plateaus: the model has learned as much as the current data supports. Try more data or a different architecture. Training loss is decreasing while validation loss starts rising: the model is overfitting. Add regularization or stop sooner. Both losses are barely moving at all from the start: the learning rate is probably too small, or there’s a more serious initialization problem. Both losses are bouncing wildly: the learning rate is almost certainly too large.
None of those diagnoses require advanced math. They require watching two lines on a graph and knowing what the shapes mean. That skill, built mostly through practice and experience with real training runs, is one of the clearest markers that separates someone who can genuinely use machine learning from someone who can only describe it.
The Habit That Separates Practitioners From Theorists
There’s one concrete practice that separates people who genuinely understand gradient descent from people who can describe it: the habit of watching loss curves during training and being able to name what they mean in real time. Not after looking up documentation in the moment, while a training run is happening.
If you can look at a plot where training loss drops smoothly while validation loss plateaus after epoch 15 and immediately think, 'This model has learned as much as the data allows. I need more data or a different architecture rather than waiting to see if the validation loss comes back down. You're thinking like a practitioner. If you see both curves oscillating wildly and immediately reduce the learning rate rather than waiting to see if it stabilizes on its own, you’re saving hours of wasted compute.
Building that intuition requires running actual training jobs and observing what happens. Not just reading about what the curves mean, but also adjusting the learning rate, watching the result change, and adjusting again. Every experiment that breaks something teaches something that reading a description of the same thing never does. The math underneath gradient descent is elegant, but the practical skill is built through deliberate observation, and that observation only comes from putting training runs in front of you and developing opinions about what you’re seeing.
This article is adapted from concepts in Machine Learning: The Complete Beginner’s Guide by Md Shafiqul Baten Sumon, available on Amazon.
